svg.text: Selection converts candidate variation into a new output distribution
Selector accounting: 340 rounds, 74 OLMo / Qwen runs. Binary-risk conversion claim: 221 transitions, held out one entire run at a time.
selector gap = kept − whole offered pool · training displacement = kept − the model’s own generated pool
Within-prompt spread = population SD (ddof=0) of the candidate value scores inside each prompt, averaged equally over prompts — not a pooled SD.
The candidate value score is 0/1 on the risk axis; the q(1−q) identity and the conversion chain hold on the binary-risk rounds only (self-report scores are continuous — see scope note).
Three distances live on one candidate-score axis
Kept minus whole pool measures the selector.
Kept minus the model’s own pool measures the update.
training displacement = pool-supply shift + selector gap (holds to 0.0001). Outside candidates create the pool-supply shift, so p ≠ q; in self-only pools p = q and the two coincide.
q own generated mean p whole offered pool mean k kept training-target mean v separate behavioral value (schematic positions)
The self-relative gap tracks behavioral movement best — and its edge grows under pool mixing
Pearson r of each distance with the round-to-round change in the separate behavioral value readout (all pools,
not just binary risk). Behavioral pull bridges to that readout — it is not a rival definition of selection.
The binary-risk chain: displacement moves the mean, the exact q(1−q) identity
Δ own mean q = 0.009 + 0.789 × training displacement (binary risk 221, r = 0.838; mixed 60: 0.028 + 0.739×, r = 0.897)
mean within-prompt variance = q(1−q) − Var(prompt means) — total binary variance is q(1−q); subtract the between-prompt part to get what selection can use inside a prompt.
Δ own spread = −0.003 + 1.405 × Δ[q(1−q)] (binary risk 221, r = 0.851; mixed 60: slope 1.359, r = 0.887)
q moves toward 0 or 1 → less next-round spread q moves toward 0.5 → more next-round spread (round number is not a term in the model)
Held out one entire run at a time, the conversion chain beats spread-persistence on
Leave-one-run-out R² predicting the model’s own next-round candidate spread; the held-out run is excluded from the fit that predicts it. Binary risk score only.
Scope: the conversion claim is binary-risk only.
The candidate value score is 0/1 on the risk axis, but continuous in [0,1] on the insecure-code
self-report axis. The 60 continuous self-report rounds (37 consecutive transitions) share the same
within-prompt spread definition for selector accounting, but sit OUTSIDE the q(1−q) conversion claim:
there the mean→spread chain scores leave-one-run-out R² −0.029 versus 0.747 for spread persistence.
Held-out ceiling: supplying the observed next mean q_next raises the exact-decomposition within-variance fit to leave-one-run-out R² 0.849 over 221
transitions, so predicting the mean update is the larger remaining bottleneck.
Sources: experiments/spread_conversion_model.json (headroom chain, persistence, drift coordinates, chain coefficients, variance split;
scripts/analysis_spread_definition_audit.py). Panel A positions are schematic; panel B uses logged rounds, panels C/D use consecutive-round
